Approval authorities for Acquisitions Program clients' programs only (other than for a Canadian Commercial Corporation)
Retroactive Treasury Board approval of contracts

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement risk assessment for complexity level 1, 2 and 3
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Procurement risk assessment for complexity level 1, 2 and 3
Procurement risk assessment for complexity level 1, 2 and 3
structure_blocks.text: Procurement risk assessment for complexity level 1, 2 and 3 A procurement risk assessment (PRA) is used to determine: the level of risk Canada is exposed to when entering into contracts the approval and signing authority levels for the procurement the foundation for risk response strategies employed to mitigate procurement risks and provide guidance to the buyer when high risks are identified As soon as the key elements of the procurement strategy have been determined, or when it is determined that Treasury Board (TB) approval is required, you must complete a PRA before preparing the approval document. The PRA process emphasizes the need to: early assessment of contract risk factors that may put a procurement at risk record risks and identify response strategies in approval documents communicate these risks to management and clients as necessary reassess risks during the procurement process when circumstances dictate and as part of ongoing monitoring and continuous improvement At any time during the procurement process, there may be a requirement to re-assess the complexity or risks in the procurement because of changing factors in the procurement or its environment. If this occurs, you must prepare a revised PRA to determine if any risk factors have changed. For complexity levels 1, 2 and 3 inclusive, the procurement risk assessments are available on the Procurement Corner – Complexity and Risk Assessment on The Source, Public Services and Procurement Canada’s intranet site (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only). For complexity levels 4 and 5, due to a high level of risk and uncertainty associated with these levels, you must contact the Risk Management Advisory Services (RMAS) group. They will work with you in completing a custom PRA specific to the complexities of a level 4 or 5 procurement. For procurements that fall under Schedule 3 of PSPC 's Common Services Acquisitions Authorities (i.e., within Departmental Authority), you must identify the risks and associated risk response measures for those risks identified as medium-high and high. You are encouraged to discuss the risk related issues with your supervisor, manager, or RMAS . For procurements requiring TB approval, you must consult RMAS to ensure all relevant medium, medium-high and high risks are identified within the TB submission document.

raw_text:
Buyers-guide-page-title-EN

Procurement risk assessment for complexity level 1, 2 and 3

Published date: [current-page:metatag:dcterms_date]

Procurement risk assessment for complexity level 1, 2 and 3 A procurement risk assessment (PRA) is used to determine: the level of risk Canada is exposed to when entering into contracts the approval and signing authority levels for the procurement the foundation for risk response strategies employed to mitigate procurement risks and provide guidance to the buyer when high risks are identified As soon as the key elements of the procurement strategy have been determined, or when it is determined that Treasury Board (TB) approval is required, you must complete a PRA before preparing the approval document. The PRA process emphasizes the need to: early assessment of contract risk factors that may put a procurement at risk record risks and identify response strategies in approval documents communicate these risks to management and clients as necessary reassess risks during the procurement process when circumstances dictate and as part of ongoing monitoring and continuous improvement At any time during the procurement process, there may be a requirement to re-assess the complexity or risks in the procurement because of changing factors in the procurement or its environment. If this occurs, you must prepare a revised PRA to determine if any risk factors have changed. For complexity levels 1, 2 and 3 inclusive, the procurement risk assessments are available on the Procurement Corner – Complexity and Risk Assessment on The Source, Public Services and Procurement Canada’s intranet site (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only). For complexity levels 4 and 5, due to a high level of risk and uncertainty associated with these levels, you must contact the Risk Management Advisory Services (RMAS) group. They will work with you in completing a custom PRA specific to the complexities of a level 4 or 5 procurement. For procurements that fall under Schedule 3 of PSPC 's Common Services Acquisitions Authorities (i.e., within Departmental Authority), you must identify the risks and associated risk response measures for those risks identified as medium-high and high. You are encouraged to discuss the risk related issues with your supervisor, manager, or RMAS . For procurements requiring TB approval, you must consult RMAS to ensure all relevant medium, medium-high and high risks are identified within the TB submission document.

Procurement risk assessment for complexity level 1, 2 and 3

A procurement risk assessment (PRA) is used to determine:

the level of risk Canada is exposed to when entering into contracts

the approval and signing authority levels for the procurement

the foundation for risk response strategies employed to mitigate procurement risks and provide guidance to the buyer when high risks are identified

As soon as the key elements of the procurement strategy have been determined, or when it is determined that Treasury Board (TB) approval is required, you must complete a PRA before preparing the approval document.

The PRA process emphasizes the need to:

early assessment of contract risk factors that may put a procurement at risk

record risks and identify response strategies in approval documents

communicate these risks to management and clients as necessary

reassess risks during the procurement process when circumstances dictate and as part of ongoing monitoring and continuous improvement

At any time during the procurement process, there may be a requirement to re-assess the complexity or risks in the procurement because of changing factors in the procurement or its environment. If this occurs, you must prepare a revised PRA to determine if any risk factors have changed.

For complexity levels 1, 2 and 3 inclusive, the procurement risk assessments are available on the Procurement Corner – Complexity and Risk Assessment on The Source, Public Services and Procurement Canada’s intranet site (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for PSPC only).

For complexity levels 4 and 5, due to a high level of risk and uncertainty associated with these levels, you must contact the Risk Management Advisory Services (RMAS) group. They will work with you in completing a custom PRA specific to the complexities of a level 4 or 5 procurement.

For procurements that fall under Schedule 3 of PSPC 's Common Services Acquisitions Authorities (i.e., within Departmental Authority), you must identify the risks and associated risk response measures for those risks identified as medium-high and high. You are encouraged to discuss the risk related issues with your supervisor, manager, or RMAS .

For procurements requiring TB approval, you must consult RMAS to ensure all relevant medium, medium-high and high risks are identified within the TB submission document.
